hub_subtitle: In the world of IoT, physical devices like electronic money cards can communicate with your phone wirelessly. Here, we explore how the PembacaKUE project works.
hub_what_is_iot: What is IoT in Simple Terms?
hub_iot_desc_1: Internet of Things (IoT) is simply a concept where physical devices (Things) are connected to each other and exchange data.
hub_iot_desc_2: In the past, the internet was only used by humans through computers or phones to send messages to each other. In the world of IoT, machines and inanimate objects also send messages. For example, an E-Toll card tells the toll gate machine: "Hey, my remaining balance is Rp 50,000, please open the gate!"
hub_iot_desc_3: In the PembacaKUE project, the "Things" that communicate are: E-Money/KMT Card and Android Phone (Balance Check App).
hub_card1_desc: How can devices connect to each other wirelessly? Let's get to know 13.56 MHz radio waves and magnetic induction.
hub_card2_desc: How can invisible waves turn into 0s and 1s that the phone understands? Understand what modulation is.
hub_card3_desc: Dissect the anatomy of the PembacaKUE project. What is the role of the Android phone and how does a smart card work behind it.
hub_card4_desc: Explore deeper into the concept of radio waves.
hub_card5_desc: Interesting facts about the development of NFC technology.
hub_card6_desc: A list of important terms in the world of IoT and Smart Cards.
nfc_title: 1. NFC Basics: The Mystery of Wireless Communication
nfc_meta: An E-Toll card can be read by your phone even without a battery. The technology behind this is NFC.
nfc_p1_1: NFC (Near Field Communication) is a short-range wireless communication technology. The distance is very close, usually only a maximum of 4 cm.
nfc_p1_2: If Bluetooth and Wi-Fi are designed to send data over long distances (several meters), NFC is designed for security and connection speed. You must intentionally tap the card to a device (such as a toll gate or phone) for communication to occur.
nfc_max_dist_desc: Very close to prevent data interception.
nfc_h3_2: The Secret of the 13.56 MHz Frequency
nfc_p2_1: In the world of radio communication, devices must be on the same "channel" or frequency to be able to talk. Just like an FM radio station (e.g., 99.0 MHz), NFC works on a specific frequency which is 13.56 MHz.
nfc_p2_2: An Android phone that has the NFC feature continuously emits radio waves on this 13.56 MHz frequency to look for a card nearby.
nfc_h3_3: Why Doesn't the E-Toll Card Have a Battery?
nfc_p3_1: Smart cards like E-Money or KMT are called Passive devices. They do not have their own battery or power source.
nfc_p3_2:
Then where does their power come from? The answer is Electromagnetic Induction.
When the card is brought close to the phone, the radio frequency waves (13.56 MHz) emitted by the phone are captured by the antenna (copper wire) coiled inside the card.
nfc_p3_3: These radio waves are then converted into small electrical energy by the chip inside the card. That electrical energy is what the chip uses to "wake up/turn on", read balance data, and reply with a message to the phone!
nfc_li_1: The phone emits a magnetic (radio) field.
nfc_li_2: The card antenna captures the magnetic field.
nfc_li_3: The magnetic field turns into electricity.
nfc_li_4: The card chip turns on without a battery!
dx_title: 2. Data Exchange: From Waves to Information
dx_meta: In addition to generating electricity, magnetic waves are used to transmit balance information.
dx_h3_1: Modulation: The Secret Language of Waves
dx_p1_1: Computers and phones only understand two numbers: 0 and 1 (Binary). To send these numbers via radio waves, the phone must change the shape of the waves. The process of changing the shape of the wave is called Modulation.
dx_p1_2: Imagine you are giving morse code using a flashlight. A bright flashlight means "1", and slightly dim means "0". In NFC, the phone changes the "strength" of its magnetic field (Amplitude) to send the numbers 0 and 1 to the card. This is called Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK).
dx_h3_2: Load Modulation: How the Card Replies
dx_p2_1: When the phone asks, the phone uses its own energy. But how does the card reply to the phone's message if the card has no battery?
dx_p2_2: The card replies using a clever technique called Load Modulation. Because the card absorbs magnetic energy from the phone, the card intentionally sucks more electrical current, then less, alternately.
dx_p2_3: The phone detects this drop in energy with a specific rhythm and reads it as the numbers 0 and 1.
dx_p3_1: Okay, the wave has turned into the numbers 1 and 0. But what does 10101011 mean? This is where conversation rules are made. In the world of Smart Cards, they chat using a standard message format called APDU (Application Protocol Data Unit).
dx_p3_2: In the PembacaKUE project, the phone acts as the commander sending a Command APDU.
dx_li_1: Phone (Command APDU): "Select the electronic money app (Select AID)!"
dx_li_3: Phone (Command APDU): "Please read the data in the balance row (Read Record)!"
dx_li_4: Card (Response APDU): "Here is the balance (Hex) and success (90 00)."
comp_meta: Here is the implementation of NFC technology in the PembacaKUE application code.
comp_p1_1: In this project, the Android phone acts as the Reader. The Android app uses the Android NFC API to access the NFC antenna hardware in the phone.
comp_p1_2: The main class used in Android is NfcAdapter and technology classes such as IsoDep or NfcA.
comp_p1_3: The phone's main tasks in this application are:
comp_li_1: Detecting when a card is tapped (Intent Action ACTION_TECH_DISCOVERED).
comp_li_2: Sending a read command (Transceive APDU) to the card.
comp_li_3: Translating the Hexadecimal reply data into identity card data or Rupiah balance.
comp_p2_1: The e-KTP or KMT card that is read is a Smart Card. Unlike ordinary memory cards, this smart card has a mini CPU (microcontroller) and its own operating system (usually Java Card OS).
comp_p2_2: This card will receive a command from the phone, process it with its own security (e.g., checking PIN or encryption keys), and only provide data if the phone sends the correct command.
comp_smartcard_desc: Has a small chip to process security data and store personal data files or balances.
comp_congrats_desc: You have completed the basic guide on how NFC works behind PembacaKUE. The processes of magnetic induction, modulation, and APDU happen every time you tap your ID card to your phone.
rw_meta: NFC, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth all operate using radio waves.
rw_p1_1: Radio waves are basically exactly the same as the sunlight you see every day. Both are Electromagnetic Waves (a combination of electric and magnetic fields moving together).
rw_p1_2: The only difference is the Frequency and Wavelength. The light we can see (like red or blue) has a very high frequency. Meanwhile, radio waves have a lower frequency, so human eyes have not evolved to see them.
rw_spectrum_desc: Gamma Rays ➔ X-Rays ➔ Visible Light ➔ Microwaves ➔ Radio Waves
rw_h3_2: History of Discovery: From Formula to Reality
rw_li_1: 1867 (Theory - James Clerk Maxwell): Mathematically, Maxwell predicted that there must be something called electromagnetic waves that propagate as fast as light, although invisible.
rw_li_2: 1887 (Proof - Heinrich Hertz): Hertz built a simple device (spark oscillator) that successfully created and detected radio waves in his laboratory. (That is why the unit of frequency is named Hertz / Hz).
rw_li_3: 1895 (Practice - Guglielmo Marconi): Marconi was the first person to make a long-distance wireless telegraph system. He successfully sent morse code across the Atlantic Ocean using radio waves.
rw_p3_1: To catch these invisible waves, we need a piece of metal called an Antenna.
rw_magic_desc: Inside a metal (antenna), there are millions of electrons that can move freely. When an electromagnetic wave (radio) hits the antenna, the magnetic and electric fields of the wave will push and pull the electrons inside the metal.
rw_p3_2: This pushing and pulling creates a microscopic alternating current (AC) in the antenna wire. The electronic circuits in our phone or radio are then tasked with detecting this small electrical current, amplifying it (Amplifier), and separating it from "noise" (Tuning / Resonance).
rw_p3_3:
This is where the connection with NFC lies!
The antenna inside your E-Toll/ID card is not straight like a radio mast, but is rolled to form a coil. When radio waves from the phone hit the metal coil in the card, the electrons inside it wiggle violently and produce an electrical current strong enough to turn on the smart chip inside without needing a battery. This law of physics is called Faraday's Law of Induction.
ht_meta: NFC technology has a long history before becoming the standard for transportation payments in Indonesia.
ht_h3_1: Evolution: From World War to Smartphones
ht_p1_1: The root of NFC is RFID (Radio Frequency Identification). A technology similar to RFID was first developed during World War II to identify friend or foe aircraft via radar waves (the British IFF system).
ht_p1_2: The first modern RFID patent was registered by Charles Walton in 1983. It wasn't until 2004 that three giant companies: Nokia, Philips, and Sony joined forces to form the "NFC Forum" to formulate a safer and more standardized NFC technology for consumers.
ht_p1_3: Did you know? The world's first phone to have NFC was the classic flip phone Nokia 6131 released in 2006! Meanwhile, for Android, this feature first appeared on the Google Nexus S in 2010.
ht_evo_desc: From military radar technology evolved into a modern payment standard in the palm of your hand.
ht_p2_1: The adoption of electronic money in Indonesia developed through several stages:
ht_li_1: 2009: E-Money was introduced widely, one of them by Bank Mandiri. But at that time, very few merchants accepted it.
ht_li_2: 2013 (KRL Commuter Line): PT KCI began implementing an electronic ticketing system (KMT - Kartu Multi Trip) replacing paper tickets. Millions of people in Jakarta and its surroundings started getting used to "Tapping" cards every day.
ht_li_3: October 2017 (Toll Gate Revolution): The Indonesian government required 100% of toll gate payments to use electronic money. This was the moment when the public massively began looking for phones with NFC features to check their e-Toll balances!
ht_trivia1_desc: The NFC card standard in Japan is called FeliCa (Felicity Card by Sony). This system is incredibly fast, capable of processing transactions in just 0.1 seconds! Because Japanese people walk very fast in train stations, their system was designed so people can walk continuously while tapping.
ht_trivia2_desc: For years since the iPhone 6, Apple locked their NFC sensor ONLY for Apple Pay services. Android users could already top up E-Toll balances, but iPhone users could not. It was only starting with iOS 13 (2019) that Apple slowly opened NFC access to read cards publicly.
ht_trivia3_desc: Because the underlying technology is electromagnetic induction, the NFC Forum in 2020 released specifications so NFC could be used to charge the batteries of small devices (like smartwatches or earphones) at power levels up to 1 Watt (WLC standard).
term_meta: Confused by technical abbreviations? Here is a cheat sheet dictionary of all the technical words we use in this project.
term_nfc_desc: Near Field Communication. A technology that allows two devices to exchange data when brought close together with a maximum distance of about 4 cm at a frequency of 13.56 MHz.
term_rfid_desc: The ancestor of NFC. Radio wave reading technology to identify objects from a long distance (can be up to hectares). For example: Mall parking gate sensors or clothing security tags in stores.
term_iot_desc: A network of physical objects ("Things") embedded with sensors, software, or other technologies to connect and exchange data with other devices/systems.
term_apdu_desc: Standard message format for talking to *Smart Cards*. Basically, this is the mandatory grammar (like a formal letter) sent by an Android phone for the card to respond. There are *Command APDUs* (commands) and *Response APDUs* (replies).
term_aid_desc: A unique number or application ID inside a *Smart Card*. In one smart card (e.g., e-KTP), there can be various applications. The phone uses AID to choose "which E-Money application to read?".
term_iso_desc: A world-class international standard (from the International Organization for Standardization) that governs how *contactless smart cards* must be made. Almost all e-money and electronic passports in the world use this standard.
term_mifare_desc: A trademark for the most popular *smart card* chip series in the world, produced by NXP Semiconductors. Very frequently used for train tickets, buses, and office door access.
term_ndef_desc: A lightweight data format specifically for NFC. Usually used on NFC stickers (NFC Tags). For example, you write an NDEF sticker containing a link to a restaurant menu, when a customer's phone taps it, the link opens directly.
term_congrats_desc: Understanding the terminology and how these components work helps you grasp the system behind smart card technology.
